sections.title: Division facts and multiplication–division relationship
sections.prose_summary: The chapter opens with an array of coconuts to establish that every multiplication fact (e.g. 5×7=35) yields exactly two division facts (35÷5=7 and 35÷7=5). Students observe that the dividend N equals the divisor D multiplied by the quotient Q (N=D×Q), grounding division as the inverse of multiplication. The section sits firmly at the concrete–pictorial level, using coconut arrays to make the relationship visible before formalising it.

tasks.description: Given 35÷1=___, write the corresponding multiplication fact and two division facts that follow.
Identify numbers to fill circles so that square values are the products or quotients of the circle numbers in a multiplication-division web.
Solve four multiplication problems (30×30, 400×8, 15×60, 200×16) and write two division statements for each.

sections.title: Patterns in division and place value
sections.prose_summary: Students explore how scaling the dividend or divisor by 10 or 100 affects the quotient, using sets like 150÷3, 1000÷10, 2000÷2, 2000÷20 and 3300÷300. A place value chart (H–T–O) makes the shift in quotient digits concrete and visible. The section sits at the pictorial–symbolic level, with the place value chart as the key representational model, and develops reasoning about multiplicative structure rather than procedural drill.

tasks.description: Solve 18 division problems arranged in scaling patterns; fill two place value (H–T–O) charts and discuss what happens to quotients in each case.
Sabina cycles 160 km in 20 days. How many kilometres does she cycle each day?
How many ₹100 notes does Seema need to buy coconuts worth ₹4200?
₹5500 distributed equally among 5 employees; what if distributed among 10? Will each get more or less? How much would be needed to give all the same amount?
Place numbers 1–8 in boxes so all four operations (÷, ×, +, −) are correct with no repetition.
Fill in 8 blanks (a–h) using the relationship N=D×Q (e.g. ___÷18=100, 870÷___=87).

sections.title: Mental strategies for division
sections.prose_summary: Two flexible mental strategies are introduced: (1) splitting the dividend into convenient parts (1248÷6 = 1200÷6 + 48÷6) including subtraction splits (1992÷4 = 2000÷4 − 8÷4); and (2) repeated halving for divisors that are powers of 2 (128÷4: halve twice). Students are asked to generate their own examples and identify when each strategy applies, placing this firmly at the symbolic level with reasoning about structure. The section develops number sense and flexible computation rather than a fixed algorithm.

tasks.description: Apply splitting and/or repeated halving strategies to solve: 64÷4, 265÷5, 1560÷8, 4824÷24, 168÷8, 144÷4 using scaffolded diagram frames.

sections.title: Long division using partial quotients and remainder
sections.prose_summary: Set on Susie and Sunitha's coconut farm, this section introduces partial quotients division (Susie's method: subtract multiples step by step) and a more efficient variant (Sunitha's method: choose a larger initial multiple to reduce steps). The remainder R is formalised with the relationship N = D×Q+R. Problems scale from 3-digit to 4-digit dividends and 2-digit divisors, all embedded in coconut farm contexts (selling, packing, oil extraction). The section operates entirely at the symbolic level.

tasks.description: Solve eight division problems using partial quotient strategies: 256÷4, 545÷5, 147÷7, 1212÷6, 648÷12, 9648÷48, 775÷25, 796÷4.

tasks.book_ref: Susie's Farm in Kerala Q1, p.124
tasks.description: 582 coconuts sold equally to 6 customers; how many each? How many bags of 25 needed for 97 coconuts? Discuss which method (Susie's or Sunitha's) is better.

tasks.book_ref: Let Us Learn to Divide, p.125
tasks.description: Complete scaffolded partial-quotient frames for 726÷4 and 902÷16; verify using N=D×Q+R and check whether 726=4×181 and 902=16×56.
Word problems: oil from 4376 coconuts (4376÷8); theatre samosa packs; buses for 342 students; Sofia's note combinations; picnic cost split; check N=D×Q+R for six division problems (a–f).

sections.title: Division using place value (standard algorithm)
sections.prose_summary: The standard short-division algorithm is developed through a place-value lens: distribute Hundreds, then Tens, then Ones, regrouping when a digit cannot be divided. Worked examples progress from 2-digit (62÷5, 75÷8) to 3-digit (324÷3, 136÷6) to 4-digit dividends (7032÷6, 3005÷5, 2874÷14, 9805÷32), with a side-by-side comparison of partial quotients and place-value methods. Students must place zeros correctly when a digit yields no quotient at that place. The section works at the symbolic level with Dienes-style block diagrams as pictorial support.

tasks.description: Complete four division problems (7032÷6, 3005÷5, 2874÷14, 9805÷32) using both partial quotient scaffold and place-value (Th–H–T–O) algorithm side by side.
Find missing divisors or dividends in 10 scaffolded division frames so there is no remainder; solve riddle: 3-digit number ÷5=42 and ×2=420.

Word problems: theatre shows for 475 people; ice cream per friend; biscuits per person per day; six division problems with remainder check; pattern sets A/B/C; cycle rally distance; identify missing information in 4 problems; bookshelf resource constraint problem.
Complete Munshi Lal's erased record book: find missing total amounts, quantities, and cost per kg for 4 vegetables using multiplication and division.

sections.title: Mathematical statements and reasoning about operations
sections.prose_summary: Students evaluate statements about arithmetic operations as always true, sometimes true, or never true, connecting division to broader number properties (odd/even sums, effects of doubling/halving, multiplying by 5). They verify equations for truth value and complete open statements. This section works entirely at the symbolic level and develops algebraic reasoning and generalisation.

tasks.description: Complete 4 statements to make them true: 7×6=___+17; 87+6=___×31; 63+___=74−4; ___÷9=16÷2.
Find 5 examples for 'odd+odd=even'; find examples or counterexamples for '×2 gives odd'; give 3 examples and 3 counterexamples for 'halving always gives even'.
Tick Always/Sometimes/Never for 5 statements about adding 10, subtraction order, doubling-halving in multiplication, odd×n=even, and multiplying by 5.
